Fetal p ancreatic and - c ell d evelopment Understanding the genetic and intrauterine environmental factors that in uence fetal pancreatic and - cell development and the contribution of maternal diabetes will provide insight into the trans - generational etiology of T2DM. Immunoreactive insulin is detectable in the human fetal pan- creas by weeks after conception and primitive islets by weeks, with evidence of functional fetal - cells by the end of the rst trimester [ ] . Increased fetal - cell stimulation and hyper- insulinemia in response to maternal hyperglycemia occurs in generated. The subsequent formation of hydrogen peroxide and hydroxyl radicals in turn leads to increased lipid peroxidation, mitochondrial swelling, DNA damage and altered expression of key genes [ ] . Antioxidative agents such as N - acetylcysteine (NAC) and the vitamins E, C and folic acid protect against dia- betes - induced malformations in rat embryos [ , , ] . Myoinositol has an important role as a precursor for a number of secondary messengers and may contribute to diabetic tera- togenesis. Cultured rodent embryos in high glucose concentra- tions have decreased inositol uptake and become inositol de cient [ ] . Inositol supplementation to embryos cultured in high glucose media or dietary addition to diabetic pregnant rodents protects against glucose - mediated malformation [ , ] . By contrast, the addition of an inositol uptake inhibitor to the culture medium of rodent embryos causes inositol de ciency and embryonic dysmorphogenesis, which is reversible if inositol is added to the culture [ ] . Antioxidants diminish both embry- onic dysmorphogenesis induced by hyperglycemia and inositol uptake inhibitors, suggesting a possible link between malforma- tions and oxidative stress [ ] . Decreased arachidonic acid and several prostaglandins, in par- ticular PGE , in diabetic embryogenesis have been implicated as a cause of malformations. Hyperglycemia during the period of neural tube closure reduces embryonic prostaglandin levels sec- ondary to altered arachidonic acid metabolism and phosphati- dylinositol turnover, and the downregulation of the embryonic COX - gene [ ] . Inhibition of rostral neural tube fusion by high glucose concentrations in rat embryos is prevented by the addition of either myoinositol or PGE , but not if indometacin, a prostaglandin synthesis inhibitor, is also added to the culture [ ] . Intraperitoneal injections of arachidonic acid to diabetic rats reduce the rates of neural tube defects (NTDs) [ ] . Reduced yolk sac PGE concentrations have been reported from human pregnancies terminated at weeks [ ] . There is a possible link between decreased prostaglandin syn- thesis and oxidative stress in cultured diabetic rat embryos; the reduced PGE concentration seen in day - rat embryos and membranes exposed to high glucose concentrations can be restored when the antioxidant NAC amide is added to the culture medium. Superoxide dismutase and NAC can prevent malforma- tions when added to the medium of rat embryos cultured in COX inhibitors, acetylsalicylic acid or high glucose concentrations [ ] . NTDs in diabetic pregnancies are times higher than in non - diabetic pregnancies [ ] . Both genetic and environmental factors are involved in NTD development [ ] . Although folic acid supplementation can prevent many cases of NTDs, the cel- lular mechanism for this protection and the relationship between maternal folate status and genetic susceptibility to NTDs are not known. Low dietary folate is an environmental factor for NTD and the dietary forti cation of enriched grain products with folic acid in the USA coincided with a % reduction in NTDs by [ ] . Human studies have not shown any evi- dence for abnormal folate metabolism in pregnant women with

Part Diabetes in Special Groups IGF - II is a placental growth factor. Fetal insulin secretion in humans occurs in response to glucose and the amino acids arginine and leucine by weeks gestation [ , ] . Maternal hyperglycemia increases placental fetal transfer of glucose and results in fetal hyperinsulineamia [ ] . The availability of amino acids and lactate is also increased in diabetic pregnancies [ ] . The diabetic intrauterine metabolic environment pro- motes abdominal fat disposition and visceral growth; notably liver, spleen and heart. This type of accelerated growth pattern is visible on routine clinical ultrasound as an increased abdominal circumference from weeks gestation (Figure . Such growth patterns are less common in hypertensive diabetic preg- nancies complicated by hypertension or vascular disease which can result in decreased uterine and placental blood ow that compromises nutrient transfer [ ] . Maternal glycemic control in uences fetal growth as early as the rst trimester as shown in a longitudinal study on preg- nancies involving women with diabetes in which an elevated rst - trimester HbA 1c level was the strongest predictor of macro- somia [ ] . Accelerated growth patterns occur through maternal hyperg- lycemia causing fetal hyperinsulinemia, as illustrated by human and animal studies [ ] . In human diabetic pregnancies there is a strong association between birth weight and fetal insulin, as assessed by amniotic and umbilical cord insulin, C - peptide and proinsulin [ , ] . Inducing hyperinsulinemia in the fetuses of healthy non - dia- betic pregnant Rhesus monkeys produces similar accelerated fetal growth to human diabetic pregnancies [ , ] . Conversely, fetal lambs rendered hypoinsulemic in utero by streptozocin have decreased somatic and skeletal growth [ ] . IGF - I is an important fetal growth factor in humans and a homozygous IGF - I gene deletion results in severe prenatal and postnatal growth restriction and mental retardation [ ] . The placental growth factor IGF - II is paternally imprinted, and func- tions to facilitate maternal fetal nutrient transfer in late preg- nancy, an effect that may depend on fetal insulin [ ] . In healthy non - diabetic pregnancies, cord IGF - I and IGF - II are correlated with birth weight [ ] . The bioavailability of the IGFs is regu- lated by the IGF binding proteins (IGFBPs) [ ] and proteases that inhibit these binding proteins are increased in human preg- nancies [ ] . Cord blood IGFBP - is lower in T2DM and GDM compared with controls without diabetes, suggesting IGF - I bio- availability is increased in diabetic pregnancies [ ] . Stillbirth Stillbirth in the UK is currently de ned as fetal loss occurring after completed weeks gestation. In both women with and without diabetes, there are identi able causes for stillbirth that include congenital malformations, chromosomal abnormalities, infection and intrauterine growth restriction. Approximately one - quarter of all cases are unexplained [ ] . The risk of still- birth in diabetic pregnancies is approximately vefold higher than for non - diabetic pregnancies [ , , ] . In the CEMACH early pregnancy and may persist throughout human pregnancy. This early priming of - cell function may explain why accelerated fetal growth patterns occur even with good metabolic control later in pregnancy [ , ] . - Cell function and insulin sensitivity in children and young adulthood are in uenced by intrauterine growth restriction [ , ] . There are critical spatiotemporal windows in embroy- onic and fetal life when maternal diet and placental function affects expression of key developmental pancreatic genes through epigenetic modi cations of DNA methylation [ ] . While most work has centered on models of growth restriction and subse- quent - cell development, in utero exposure to hyperglycemia also affects fetal, neonatal and adult - cell number and function in rodents [ ] ; however, these experimental models may be less relevant to human pancreatic - cell development than those in larger mammals, as the rodent fetal endocrine pancreas is more immature in utero and - cells continue to proliferate postnatally. The molecular changes linking growth restriction and - cell mass in the sheep include a normal - cell mass in fetal and early adult life, corresponding with upregulation of the genes for IGF - II and insulin gene as well as their receptors. By late adult life, however, male sheep have reduced - cell mass and develop diabetes having lost their ability to upregulate these genes and maintain an adequate - cell mass [ ] . Pancreatic duodenal homeobox - (Pdx1) is a pancreatic tran- scription factor that regulates pancreas development and - cell differentiation. Decreased Pdx1 expression in humans is associ- ated with T2DM in adulthood. Reduced fetal Pdx1 expression secondary to epigenetic modi cation occurs in growth - restricted rodents and remains reduced into adulthood, suggesting the window for epigenetic modi cation of - cell gene expression extends beyond the embryonic period [ , ] . Abnormal f etal g rowth Factors in uencing fetal growth include maternal fetal nutrient transfer, maternal weight and nutritional status [ ] , placental size, uterine blood ow, and fetal and parental genes [ ] . In a healthy non - diabetic pregnancy, parental and fetal genes will be the major contributor to birth weight, while the fetal metabolic intrauterine environment is also a major in uence in diabetic pregnancies. The infant of a mother with diabetes is often growth promoted [ ] , a nding con rmed in the CEMACH audit, in which % of infants were above the 90th percentile for gesta- tional age [ ] . An analysis of singleton babies born after weeks gestation to mothers with T1DM in Scotland, , found birth weight to be . SD above the population norm, an increase that remained unchanged over the years of the study [ ] . The potential consequences of accelerated growth in utero include an increased risk of emergency cesarean section, birth trauma and birth asphyxia [ , ] as well as future childhood and adult obesity. Glucose is the major fetal and placental fuel substrate and fetal insulin and IGF - I are both fetal anabolic hormones [ ] , while

Part Diabetes in Special Groups occurs in approximately half of all diabetic pregnancies [ ] . Capillary blood glucose readings are often unreliable as many glucose sticks in routine clinical use are not quality assured and validated for neonatal use. It is therefore recommended that ward - based glucose electrode or laboratory analysis is performed to con rm hypoglycemia. The working de nition for neonatal hypoglycemia is a blood glucose value < . mmol/L [ , ] . In the majority of healthy neonates, a low blood glucose concentration is merely a normal physiologic response to the metabolic adaptation to extrauterine life [ ] . Macrosomic infants are at higher risk of hypoglycemia because of postpartum hyperinsulinemia secondary to in utero - cell hyperplasia. This risk of hypoglycemia is further increased because hyperinsulinemia inhibits hepatic glucose production. Hypoglycemia may also be aggravated by polycythemia (second- ary to increased glucose uptake by red blood cells), and possibly by impaired glucagon release. Preterm infants have more variable swings in blood glucose and are therefore more at risk of hypoglycemia [ ] and this may affect subsequent mental and motor development [ ] . Early feeding reduces the risk of hypoglycemia [ ] and it is therefore recommended that all mothers with diabetes are encouraged to breastfeed within minutes of birth and then every hours, until a regular feeding pattern is established. risk factor for stillbirth [ , ] . Other identi able risk factors in the Danish study were diabetic nephropathy, smoking and lower social status [ ] . Obesity and increasing maternal age are other risk factors for stillbirth in the non - diabetic population [ , ] . Among obstetric units that serve extremely obese T2DM women (e.g. South Paci c Islanders), the stillbirth risk is higher in those with T2DM than T1DM [ , ] . As obesity and T2DM trends con- tinue to rise among women of reproductive years, differences in stillbirth rates between women with T2DM and T1DM may become more apparent in women of Northern European ancestry. The cause for the excess stillbirths in diabetic pregnancies that cannot be attributable to congenital malformations and other identi able causes remains uncertain, although chronic fetal hypoxia and acidosis appear to be major contributory factors [ ] . Fetal hyperinsulinemia combined with the availability of excessive fuel substrate increases oxygen demand in insulin sensi- tive tissues and this may exceeds placental oxygen supply [ ] . Human and large animal studies suggest that the fetuses of dia- betic pregnancies are more susceptible to acidosis than those of a non - diabetic pregnancy [ ] . Amniotic erythropoietin is a marker of chronic fetal hypoxia in late pregnancy and is higher in diabetic than non - diabetic pregnancies [ ] . In one study of women with T1DM, amniotic erythropoietin levels correlated inversely with cord blood pH and pO at birth and neonatal hypoglycemia and were positively correlated with maternal HbA 1c levels in late pregnancy [ ] . The clinical potential of using fetal amniotic uid erythro- poietin levels to identify high - risk pregnancies needs to be assessed in clinical trials. Another potential factor that could have a minimal affect on fetal hypoxia in late diabetic pregnancies is the greater oxygen af nity of glycated hemoglobin compared with non - glycated hemoglobin which could theoretically impair oxygen delivery to the fetus. The n eonate Neonates born to mothers with diabetes have a higher risk of being born preterm (less than weeks gestation), being large for gestational age, having a congenital malformation or birth injury and developing hypoglycemia requiring treatment. Neonatal admission to a special care baby unit is common although this often re ects hospital policy rather than clinical need [ ] . Approximately half of all neonates born to women with diabe- tes are large for gestational age (above the 90th percentile) [ ] . The increase in subcutaneous fat distribution in these infants combined with their high hematocrit gives them a typical mac- rosomic appearance with plethoric features and an obese body (Figure . Hypoglycemia is the most common, albeit usually transient, metabolic condition in infants of women with diabetes and Figure . Left: A macrosomic baby born to a mother with diabetes. Right: A normal baby born to a mother without diabetes.

Diabetes in Pregnancy Chapter are at increased risk of developing T2DM or IGT in middle age [ ] , suggesting that developing - cells are programmed by intrauterine events including nutrition and hyperglycemia [ ] . Risks of p regnancy for the m other with d iabetes Pregnancy may affect pre - existing microvascular and macrovas- cular disease but does not usually have any lasting detrimental effect on either retinopathy or nephropathy. In contrast, preg- nancy poses a risk for women with established cardiovascular disease, and this risk should be discussed in older women and those with a long duration of disease. Microvascular c omplications Retinopathy Diabetic retinopathy is more common in women with T1DM of childbearing age than T2DM [ ] . It is not a contraindication to pregnancy but retinopathy may progress during pregnancy, espe- cially in women with moderate or severe retinopathy before preg- nancy [ , ] . Nevertheless, it rarely has any long - term detrimental effect on the natural history or progression of dia- betic retinopathy [ , ] . An analysis of women with T1DM before pregnancy and yearly follow - up for years post preg- nancy, with - year follow - up data available for women, showed baseline retinopathy status was the only independent risk factor that predicted later progression of retinopathy [ ] . Decreased retinal blood ow and retinal arterial constriction occur in the third trimester of a healthy pregnancy, but are more pronounced in diabetic pregnancies [ ] . These changes, com- bined with pregnancy - related hypervolemia and hypercoagula- tion, could theoretically lower the threshold for retinal ischemia and hypoxia and hence progression of retinopathy. Pregnancy is associated with many circulating growth factors, angiopoietic factors and pro - in ammatory markers, but to date none have been clearly associated with the progression of retinopathy in pregnancy [ ] . Retinopathy can develop de novo in pregnancy [ ] , although this is unlikely to progress to proliferative retinopathy [ ] . Progression to proliferative retinopathy is also uncommon among women with mild or minimal retinopathy at booking. In a prospective study of pregnancies among women with T1DM followed between and , progression to prolifera- tive retinopathy requiring laser therapy only occurred in . Proliferative changes occurred in only two women, one who had occasional microaneurysms at booking and the other who had fewer than four microaneuryms per eye [ ] . Progression to proliferative retinopathy requiring laser treatment is more common in women with more advanced retinopathy at booking and longer duration of diabetes [ , , ] . Laser treatment before pregnancy protects against proliferative changes in pregnancy [ ] . Hypertension in uence retinopathy progres- The current NICE guidelines only recommend the use of intra- venous glucose to treat neonatal hypoglycemia when there are clinical signs of hypoglycemia or a persistent blood sugar < . mmol/L that cannot be raised by oral or tube feeding, or when the blood glucose is very low ( . If intrave- nous glucose is required, this should be continued until the glucose is > . Other transient neonatal complications include polycythemia resulting from enhanced antepartum hemapoiesis [ ] in response to fetal hyperinsulinemia [ ] and chronic fetal tissue hypoxia [ ] . Respiratory distress syndrome was considered to occur more often in diabetic pregnancies but is likely to result from preterm birth and high rate of cesarean section rather than any underlying metabolic effect [ ] . Transient hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, characterized by ven- tricular septal hypertrophy and subaortic stenosis, occurs in up to % of all babies of mothers with diabetes [ , ] . Congestive cardiac failure presents in only % of babies and this usually has a benign course resolving within a month and without any clini- cal sequelae. Both hypocalcemia and hypomagnesemia occur more fre- quently in the infant of a mother with diabetes, usually as a consequence of prematurity or birth asphyxia. Jaundice is more common in macrosomic babies. The cause of hyperbilirubinemia is probably multifactorial including birth trauma, polycythemia, hemolysis and immature hepatic uptake and conjugation of bilirubin. Increased r isk of d iabetes in l ater l ife Both T1DM and T2DM are the result of environmental factors operating in a genetically susceptible individual [ , ] . Diabetes in pregnancy can in uence the incidence of obesity and diabetes in the offspring [ , ] . Maternal hyperglycemia during pregnancy predisposes to an earlier presentation of T2DM in the child than when diabetes develops in the mother after the pregnancy [ ] . Children and young adults of mothers with T1DM are also more insulin resistant and glucose intolerant than children of mothers without diabetes [ ] . Similar meta- bolic abnormalities are not seen in the young adults of fathers with T1DM [ ] . An in uence of the intrauterine environment on the susceptibility to T2DM is also suggested by the observation that a family history of diabetes is more often reported on the maternal than the paternal side of the family [ ] . Studies in the Pima population on sibling pairs born before and after the mother had developed diabetes has con rmed the importance of the intrauterine exposure to the risk of future T2DM [ ] ; over % of children with a prenatal exposure to maternal diabetes have themselves developed diabetes by years. A prenatal exposure to maternal hyperglycemia is believed to be responsible for % of all T2DM in the Pima children ( years) [ , ] . Growth restriction and intrauterine nutritional deprivation predispose to diabetes in later life [ ] . Small - for - dates babies

Part Diabetes in Special Groups Women with macroalbuminuria or proteinuria (de ned as ACR > mg/mmol or urinary albumin concentration > mg/L) are at increased risk of accelerated deterioration of renal function during pregnancy. The risk of proteinuria on maternal and fetal health increases with increasing proteinuria and declin- ing kidney function. Pre - eclampsia is common within this group. In a prospective study of women with T1DM, pre - eclampsia developed in % of women without microalbuminuria, in % with microalbuminuria and % of women with proteinuria [ ] . Women whose protein excretion exceeds g/day or whose serum creatinine concentration exceeds mol/L before preg- nancy require pre - pregnancy advice regarding the risk to them- selves and any future unborn child. This should be given by an appropriate specialist who can provide prognostic information on diabetic nephropathy as well as advice on hypertensive man- agement and thromboprophylaxis [ ] . Overall survival rates for diabetic nephropathy continue to improve. The Danish National Register on Dialysis and Transplantation reported an increased - year overall survival rate of % between and [ ] . These women need to receive realistic and current information on their kidney disease to help them make informed choices to balance a potentially shortened life expectancy and impaired quality of life with the responsibilities of motherhood. A study of kidney function and end - stage renal replacement requirement in Danish women with T1DM and diabetic neph- ropathy between and followed for years found that the renal outcomes of the women who became pregnant were similar to those who had not become pregnant [ ] ; however, nine mothers had died during the study period when their chil- dren were between and years old (median years), and a further ve required renal replacement therapy [ ] . Diabetic nephropathy, especially when the serum creatinine is raised, carries a signi cant risk of preterm birth before weeks gestation, very low birth weight and neonatal hypoglycemia [ ] . Early intensive management of hypertension can lessen this risk [ ] . The increased risk of preterm births probably accounts for the mild disturbances in growth and development in early childhood seen in offspring of these pregnancies. A - year follow - up study from birth of children of mothers with dia- betes and pre - gestational proteinuria and children of mothers without proteinuria found the children of mothers with neph- ropathy were signi cantly smaller, had mild linguistic develop- mental delay and greater susceptibility to infections [ ] . The number of women with diabetes undergoing either a renal transplant or a combined renal pancreatic transplant is increas- ing. The rst report of successful pregnancies following a renal transplant was in [ ] . Successful pregnancies following a transplant are now common [ ] , with three large registries providing pregnancy outcome data. The National Transplantation Pregnancy Registry in the USA, the European Dialysis and Transplant Association Registry and the UK Transplant Pregnancy Registry all show some increased risk of miscarriages, stillbirths, sion and optimal blood pressure control is therefore important [ ] . Poor glycemic control in early pregnancy and rapid improve- ment of glycemic control are associated with retinopathy progres- sion [ ] . In the DCCT, women with T1DM had pregnancies. All women originally assigned to conventional insulin treatment were switched to intensive therapy in early pregnancy. Compared with non - pregnant control subjects in the intensively treated arm, there was a . - fold increased risk of retinopathy deterioration in pregnancy, while the deterioration in the women originally assigned to conventional treatment was . - fold higher than in the non - pregnant control group. The previously conventionally treated group also had a . - fold increased risk of a three - step progression or more from the base- line retinopathy level than the non - pregnant control group, with the greatest risk occurring in the second trimester ( . - fold increase) and persisting for months postpartum. Although individual subjects had transient worsening of retin- opathy during pregnancy by the end of the DCCT, levels of retin- opathy in subjects who had become pregnant were similar to those who had not, for each treatment group. The worsening of retinopathy in pregnancy in the previously conventionally treated group was attributed to the women entering pregnancy with poor glycemic control experiencing a rapid improvement in their gly- cemic control [ ] . A similar conclusion that rapid intensi ca- tion of glycemic control was a risk factor for progression of retinopathy was observed in the Diabetes in Early Pregnancy Study [ ] . These studies highlight the importance of optimiz- ing glycemic control prior to pregnancy. Annual retinal screening with digital camera is now part of routine diabetic clinical care, and there is evidence that its intro- duction into clinical practice is improving the natural history of diabetic retinopathy and reducing blindness [ ] . Diabetic n ephropathy Normal pregnancy causes a physiologic increase in glomerular ltration rate (GFR) and creatinine clearance. Estimated GFR formulas based on the Modi cation of Diet in Renal Disease (MDRD) study equation or the Cockcroft Gault formulae are consequently unreliable during pregnancy. Pregnancy is not associated with deteriorating kidney function in most women with diabetes. Among women with established moderate to advanced nephropathy, pregnancy may be associated with a deterioration in kidney function; however, this usually reverts to pre - pregnancy levels in the postpartum period [ ] . Women with normal urinary albumin : creatinine ratios (ACR < . mg/mmol) and serum creatinine ( < mol/L) seldom experience any adverse effect on renal function during pregnancy. Women with microalbuminuria are not at risk of deteriorating renal function during pregnancy, but are at risk of pregnancy - induced hypertension, pre - eclampsia and preterm birth; early intensive hypertensive management in pregnancy may reduce these risks [ ] , as can good glycemic control [ ] .

Diabetes in Pregnancy Chapter Co - m orbidities Obesity Obesity is a common co - morbidity in women with diabetes. The antenatal population is becoming more obese, re ecting the secular trend in obesity. It has a major impact on maternal and fetal health as well as on health services and resources [ ] . While one expects the majority of women with T2DM and GDM to be obese, an increasing number of women with T1DM are also overweight or obese [ ] . In the CEMACH enquiry, % of the women with T2DM and % of the women with T1DM were obese [ ] . Maternal and infant mortality [ ] increases with increasing maternal BMI. The risk of congenital malformations and still- birth are increased [ ] . Obesity increases the risk of pre - eclampsia, GDM [ ] , the need for induction of labor, emergency cesarean section rates, postpartum hemorrhage and infection [ ] . Babies of obese mothers are more likely to have birth weight above the 90th percentile [ ] and be at risk of birth trauma and adult obesity and diabetes [ ] . The rst report linking pre - gestational weight and malforma- tions in South Wales between and found that mothers of anencephalic infants were signi cantly heavier than a matched control group [ ] . Subsequent epidemiologic and cohort studies have con rmed this nding. A population - based case control study of mothers of infants born with and without selected birth defects in metropolitan areas of Atlanta, USA, between and showed for every incremental unit increase in BMI, the odds ratio for a major malformation increased by % [ ] . The most common malformations reported occurring as either single or multiple defects, were NTDs, especially spina bi da, omphalocele and heart defects [ ] . Although cardiac malformations and NTD are increased in babies of women with diabetes, obtaining euglycemia in obese women with diabetes may not fully protect them against the added risk of a congenital malformation. A cohort study of infants born to mothers with GDM showed that both diabetes severity and pre - pregnancy BMI were predictors of the % minor and . % major malformations, with the BMI being the main contributor [ ] . Data from pregnant women enrolled in a prospective cohort study reported that major non - chromo- somal congenital defects associated with diabetes were more common in obese women [ ] . A study of Spanish mothers with GDM also reported that BMI kg/m was associated with a two - to threefold greater risk of cardiovascular defects compared with non - obese women [ ] . Maternal obesity and GDM may increase the risk of CNS birth defects through shared metabolic mechanisms. Autoimmune d iseases Women with T1DM are at increased risk of other autoimmune disease, especially thyroid disease and celiac disease. Thyroid function should be monitored in pregnant women with hypothy- roidism as small increases in thyroxine replacement are frequently ectopic pregnancies, preterm births, low birth weight babies and neonatal deaths [ ] . Since , the UK has had a dedicated transplant pregnancy registry and live birth rates among renal transplant recipients now approach % [ ] . Among renal transplant recipients, pregnancy was not associated with any long - term adverse effect on the transplant. When the pre - pregnancy serum creatinine was > mol/L, however, there was a tendency for the serum cre- atinine to be higher postpartum. The miscarriage rate, intrauter- ine fetal loss before weeks gestation and stillbirth rate were %, % and %, respectively. Approximately half of the births occurred before weeks, with a mean gestation of . Hypertension occurred in %, with pre - eclampsia diagnosed in approximately one - third, although this may be over - reported as the diagnosis of pre - clampsia is dif cult in women with pre - existing hypertension, renal impairment and proteinuria. The Toronto Renal Transplant Program is one of the few studies to report follow - up data on children born to renal transplant recipients. Postnatal growth was normal although developmental assessment showed one child had moderate to severe sensorineural hearing loss, another a learning disability and a third a pervasive developmental disorder [ ] . It is likely in the future that more women with diabetic neph- ropathy will have a combined pancreas kidney transplant before a planned pregnancy. The bene t and risk of a combined pan- creas kidney transplant over a single kidney transplant has not yet been evaluated for pregnancy. Macrovascular c omplications As the general antenatal population becomes more obese and more women give birth in their late reproductive years, ischemic heart disease (IHD) in pregnancy will become more prevalent [ ] . Maternal diabetes, obesity and increasing age are all iden- ti able risk factors for IHD in pregnancy [ , ] . Pregnancy increases the risk of an acute myocardial infarction (MI) three - to fourfold, with the risk being greatest in the peri- partum period [ ] . The number of pregnant women with IHD and maternal death from IHD has increased in recent years; IHD now represents the most common cause of cardiac death associated with pregnancy in the UK [ ] . Nevertheless, the mortality rates from acute MI in pregnancy have declined [ , ] . Women with diabetes have a twofold higher risk of dying from IHD than women without diabetes [ , ] . Population studies that capture data on % of the UK population at any one time have reported that women with T1DM, aged years, have a - fold higher risk of a major CVD event [ ] and women with T2DM, aged years, have a vefold higher risk of an MI than women without diabetes of a similar age [ ] . The incidence and maternal mortality rate from acute MI in pregnancy was reported to be in deliveries and . % respectively in California between and [ ] ; however, the mortality rate from MI among pregnant women with diabetes is likely to be higher than it is in non - pregnant women [ ] .

Part Diabetes in Special Groups Accessing p re - p regnancy c are Some % of women will attend a clinic for pre - pregnancy advice, but this attendance depends on the woman s perception of its bene ts [ , , ] . A study from Michigan, USA, found half of pregnant women with diabetes who had not received pre - pregnancy care claimed they had not been informed of its impor- tance [ ] . Women who seek out and engage in pre - pregnancy care tend to have higher educational and economic status and greater family and social support [ , ] . Unplanned pregnancies are common among women with dia- betes. In the CEMACH enquiry, only % and % of women with T1DM and T2DM, respectively, had planned their preg- nancy [ ] . % of the women had received pre - pregnancy care: . % of the women with T1DM and . % of the women with T2DM. A survey in New Zealand showed % of non - pregnant women with diabetes aged years would like more information about diabetes and pregnancy [ ] , suggesting there is an unmet need for good pre - pregnancy advice. It remains a clinical chal- lenge to increase the awareness and uptake of pre - pregnancy care for all women with diabetes planning pregnancy, especially among more hard to reach groups. Information to c over d uring p re - p regnancy c are All pre - pregnancy advice should be supported by evidence of its bene t in pregnancy. The advice should be constructive and non - judgmental, and communicated in a way that is appropriate for the individual woman [ ] . Women should receive information that covers the following topics: The bene ts of optimal glycemic control and appropriate and safe glycemic targets; Folic acid supplementation; General lifestyle advice (e.g. smoking and alcohol); Advice on diet and exercise; Advice on safe prescription medications; General information of pregnancy risk to the woman s health; General information of pregnancy risk to the fetus, neonate and their long - term health; and General information on diabetic pregnancy management guidelines and the need for extra surveillance. Bene ts of o ptimal g lycemic c ontrol p rior to c onception The bene ts of optimal glycemic control prior to conception on pregnancy outcomes should be explained, stressing the need to continue contraception prior to achieving the agreed glycemic target. While it is important to discuss the strong association between poor pre - conception glycemic control and congenital malformations, this risk should not be exaggerated as malforma- tions only occur in a minority of diabetic pregnancies and may discourage some women in engaging in pre - pregnancy care [ ] . There is clinical evidence that pre - pregnancy care is successful in improving glycemic control in the rst trimester and reduces required [ ] . This is important as there is some evidence linking mild degrees of hypothyroidism in the rst trimester with adverse neurodevelopment [ ] . The simultaneous ingestion of iron and thyroxine may inhibit thyroxine absorption, and women should be advised to take any iron supplements at least hours after or before taking thyroxine [ ] . Women with a previous history of Graves disease will also need their thyroid function measured before and during pregnancy. Fetal monitoring is also recommended as maternal thyroid - stim- ulating antibodies can cross the placenta causing transient fetal and neonatal hyperthyroidism [ ] . Clinical m anagement Pre - p regnancy c are Pregnancy care for women with diabetes should begin prior to conception when glycemic control can be optimized, medication reviewed and folic acid started. All health care providers who look after women with diabetes should emphasize the bene ts of pre - pregnancy planning and good glycemic control on pregnancy outcomes and encourage women to plan their pregnancies and to engage in pre - pregnancy care. Discussions about contraception and pregnancy plans should form part of the ongoing care of all pre - menopausal women with diabetes. Once a woman expresses an interest in becoming preg- nant she should have access to a pre - pregnancy clinic where spe- ci c pre - conceptual advice can be given. Evidence of e ffectiveness There have been no randomized clinical trials of pre - pregnancy counseling nor are there ever likely to be. The evidence of effectiveness is therefore based predominately on observational studies of self - selected women. Furthermore, there is no stand- ardized de nition of what constitutes pre - pregnancy care [ ] . Nevertheless, pregnancy outcomes are better in women with dia- betes who plan their pregnancies [ ] . One Scottish study ana- lyzed adverse pregnancy outcomes by markers of pregnancy planning and pre - pregnancy care in singleton T1DM preg- nancies. The best pregnancy outcomes were in women who achieved an optimal HbA 1c level before conceiving. These women were ve times less likely to have an unfavorable outcome than women with suboptimal HbA 1c levels prior to conception [ ] . Pre - conception care has been shown through economic health care models to be cost effective [ , ] . Clinical studies have shown that planned pregnancies are associated with improved glycemic control in early pregnancy and fewer congenital malformations. A Dutch study of women with T1DM who became pregnant showed a signi - cantly lower incidence of major congenital malformations among the women who had planned their pregnancies ( . % [ / ] vs . % [ / ]); relative risk . , ( % CI .

Part Diabetes in Special Groups ipine, amlodipine, hydralzine and labetalol. Beta - blockers are usually avoided in the rst trimester because of the risk of growth restriction. Other antihypertensive agents should be used on an individual basis and the risks and bene ts reviewed. Two small cohort studies suggest calcium - channel blockers are not tera- togenic [ , ] . The use of oral hypoglycemic agents other than metformin and glibenclamide are not recommended in pregnancy. Women on oral hypoglycemic therapy during pre - pregnancy care should be switched to insulin if their HbA 1c values are above target, although metformin can be continued. General i nformation on d iabetic p regnancy m anagement g uidelines and the n eed for e xtra s urveillance During pre - pregnancy care, women should be informed of the need to access antenatal care as soon as a pregnancy has been con rmed and the need for additional recommended clinic attendances and fetal monitoring above those for routine ante- natal care. The increased fetal monitoring includes ultrasound scanning to assess fetal viability and dating in early pregnancy as well as fetal growth in late pregnancy. General information con- cerning timing and mode of birth can be given at this time. Screening for d iabetic c omplications Women can be reassured that although diabetic retinopathy and nephropathy may progress, it is rare for pregnancy to have long - term detrimental effects. Screening for retinopathy or nephropa- thy prior to pregnancy is important to assess if any treatment is required before pregnancy and if greater surveillance or treat- ment will be required during pregnancy. The CEMACH enquiry found that women with T2DM were less likely to have a retinal examination and an assessment of albuminuria in the year prior to pregnancy than women with T1DM [ ] . Screening for r etinopathy All women seeking pregnancy should be informed of the impor- tance of retinal screening and the bene ts it offers. Any woman with known proliferative retinopathy should be considered for laser treatment prior to pregnancy. Women actively seeking preg- nancy should ideally have a dilated digital retinal photograph if one has not been performed in the preceding months. Women should be informed of the retinal screening guidelines for pregnancy, which include a rst trimester screen and a repeat screen at weeks if no retinopathy is present, with an additional screen at weeks if retinopathy is present [ ] . Women should be reassured that the use of tropicamide to dilate the eye is safe in pregnancy, as is photocoagulation therapy if required. Women who have or develop retinopathy in pregnancy should be aware that retinal follow - up should continue for at least months postpartum [ ] . Worsening of retinopathy in pregnancy may be associated with rapid intensi cation of glycemic control [ , ] , empha- sizing the importance of optimizing glycemic control prior to pregnancy. with pre - gestational diabetes, women who smoked during the preconception period had an increase risk of a stillbirth, neonatal death or congenital malformation (OR . ) [ , ] . Advice about alcohol should be similar to the general popula- tion, which is to avoid alcohol in the rst months of pregnancy [ ] . For women with diabetes, this is especially important as alcohol can increase the risk of hypoglycemia and is associated with an increased risk of miscarriage. Advice on d iet and e xercise Pre - pregnancy is a time to provide dietary information that will optimize glycemic control and minimize hypoglycemia during intensive glycemic management. Obese women should be encour- aged to achieve a healthy weight prior to pregnancy through diet and exercise if they have BMI > kg/m [ , ] . Advice on s afe p rescription m edications Medication should be reviewed prior to conception. The most common drugs that should be stopped are antihypertensive drugs acting on the renin - angiotensin system, lipid - lowering drugs and certain hypoglycemic oral agents. The continuation of long - act- ing analog insulin needs to be discussed on an individual basis. Angiotensin - converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors cross the placenta [ ] . Although the use of ACE inhibitors in the rst trimester of pregnancy was initially felt to be safe [ ] , there is suf cient evidence to recommend their discontinuation before conception. First trimester exposure to ACE inhibitors was linked to mal- formations in a study of infants with no drug exposure and infants exposed to ACE inhibitors but no other drugs in the rst trimester from . The risk ratio of major congenital malformations, primarily cardiovascular and CNS anomalies in women who took ACE inhibitors was . ) compared with infants who had no drug exposure [ ] . There is little information on the teratogenic effects of the angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs). Fetal exposure to losartan at weeks gestation has been linked with oligohydramnios and the intrauterine death of a fetus with hypoplastic lungs and skull bones [ ] . A series of ve cases exposed to ARBs found one term baby with additional digits, two pregnancies compli- cated with oligohydramnios and premaurity, another infant with transient abnormal renal function and two babies delivered with no abnormalities [ ] . Current recommendations are to avoid ARBs in pregnancy [ , , ] . The HMGCoA reductase inhibitors, statins, are commonly used in diabetic care. A small number of case reports and surveil- lance registers have reported congenital malformations following rst trimester statin exposure [ , ] . Current guidelines rec- ommend that statins are not to be taken during pregnancy or lactation [ ] . Methyldopa remains the rst - line antihypertensive agent in pregnancy, but other agents considered to be safe include nifed-

Diabetes in Pregnancy Chapter In clinical practice the limitations in achieving normoglycemia are the high risk of severe hypoglycemia in women intensively treated with insulin and growth restriction in a fetus of a woman too aggressively treated with hypoglycemic management or dietary restriction. For women with T1DM, glycemic control is dependent on balancing insulin requirements with the physiologic changes of pregnancy and the risk of hypoglycemia. This can only be achieved through frequent glucose monitoring and exible insulin dosing, using either a basal bolus regimen or insulin pump. Women with T2DM will also require intensive insulin man- agement alongside diet and exercise to achieve the required level of glycemic control for pregnancy. For women with GDM, approximately half will be able to achieve the desired glycemic target through diet and exercise, with the remainder requiring the addition of an appropriate oral hypoglycemic agent or insulin. Risk of h ypoglycemia Insulin sensitivity increases in the rst trimester before declining in the second. Insulin requirements for women who become pregnant with good glycemic control will usually fall in the rst trimester before rising in the mid - second trimester [ , ] . This initial increase in insulin sensitivity occurs at a time when there is a physiologic fall in fasting blood glucose and makes women with T1DM extremely susceptible to hypoglycemia. This may be further exaggerated by a pregnancy - induced decrease in counter - regulatory hormones to hypoglycemia [ , ] . In a prospective observational study of consecutive Danish pregnant women with T1DM, self - reported episodes of hypogly- cemia, validated by structured interview within hours, occurred in women ( %) experiencing severe hypoglycemic events requiring third party assistance. Of these severe episodes, % occurred in the rst weeks of pregnancy and approximately half occurred at night; women had ve or more events. Predictors for severe hypoglycemia were a history of similar events in the preceding year, impaired hypoglycemia awareness and long duration of T1DM. There was no evidence that hypogly- cemia unawareness changed during the pregnancy or that preg- nancy - related nausea was a signi cant risk factor [ ] . In an earlier Danish study in the rst trimester, nocturnal hypoglyc- emia, de ned as blood glucose values < . mmol/L, was detected in of women with T1DM who had hourly blood glucose sampling during the night ( . These episodes of hypoglycemia went unrecognized by all but one of the women despite lasting hours (mean . In the CEMACH enquiry, % of women with T1DM had recurrent hypoglycemia during pregnancy, with / ( %) episodes requiring external help. While women with T2DM are less likely to experience recurrent or severe hypoglycemia, one - fth of women with T2DM had recurrent hypoglycemia, with / ( %) requiring external help [ ] . Nocturnal hypoglycemia has also been well documented in insulin - treated pregnant women with T2DM undergoing continuous glucose monitoring [ ] . Screening for n ephropathy Women actively seeking pregnancy should be screened for micro- albuminuria and have their serum creatinine measured. Women with a normal urinary ACR ( < . mg/mmol) and serum creati- nine < mol/L can be reassured that their kidney function poses no added risk to the pregnancy. Women with microalbu- minuria (ACR . mg/mmol) need to be aware that although pregnancy will not adversely affect their renal function long - term, they are at an increased risk for hypertension and pre - eclampsia in pregnancy [ ] . For women with macroalbuminuria or proteinuria and established nephropathy, pre - pregnancy advice should cover the evidence that is discussed above. Screening for autoimmune disorders Thyroid function should be assessed pre - pregnancy as autoim- mune hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism are more common in women with diabetes and both conditions can affect the fetus and neonate [ , ] . Women with T1DM and celiac disease seeking pregnancy need to be aware that compliance with a glu- ten - free diet helps fertility rates and during pregnancy lessens the risk of anemia [ ] . Ischemic heart disease Ischemic heart disease in pregnancy is associated with serious mortality and morbidity for the mother and child. Women with a previous or suspected history of IHD should be referred and assessed by a cardiologist prior to pregnancy. Women with T1DM of long duration and older women with T2DM should be informed of their underlying risk for IHD and the added risk this has during pregnancy. A resting and an exercise electrocardio- gram (ECG) may be required prior to pregnancy to assess this risk so an informed decision can be made concerning any future pregnancies. For women known to have IHD the potential mor- tality associated with pregnancy should be discussed. Antenatal m anagement Once pregnancy is con rmed women should have easy and immediate access to a combined diabetic and obstetric clinic that can manage and support them throughout the pregnancy. This clinic should comprise a diabetologist with an interest in obstet- rics, an obstetrician with an interest in diabetes, a diabetes spe- cialist nurse/educator, a midwife with an interest in diabetes and a dietitian. The rst clinic visit needs to ensure that the woman is taking folic acid and has glycemic management optimized. This will require most women with pre - gestational diabetes not on an insulin pump to be switched to a basal bolus regimen, if not already on one. Glycemic m anagement Maternal hyperglycemia from the time of conception to birth affects pregnancy outcome. The aim of glycemic management is to achieve glycemic control as near to that of women without diabetes as possible with due consideration to safety.

Part Diabetes in Special Groups Glycemic t argets in p regnancy Glycemic targets for pregnancy have been set by national guide- lines, and are the same for all women with diabetes regardless of its type; however, there is a need to adjust and discuss these targets individually according to what is safe and achievable for the woman. This is especially true for women with T1DM who experience recurrent hypoglycemia during the rst trimester. The current targets for the UK are fasting blood glucose of . mmol/L and hour post - prandial blood glucose < . mmol/L, if safely achievable [ ] . In women with GDM, active glycemic management with diet, exercise and insulin, if required, using the targets of a fasting blood glucoses < . mmol/L and a - hour post - prandial level < . mmol/L was associated with less perinatal morbidity than no active glycemic management ( % vs %; RR . ) in a large randomized trial of women [ ] . Too stringent targets for glycemic control in women with GDM may be potentially detrimental for the infant [ ] . In a prospective study of women with GDM who were rand- omized to maintaining their mean blood glucose levels to < . mmol/L or to > . mmol/L, there was a twofold higher incidence of small for gestational age infants ( %) in the lowest target group compared with a well - matched control group of women without diabetes. In the high glucose group, an approximate twofold increased incidence of large for gestational age infants was seen, while when mean blood glucose values were maintained at . mmol/L, the incidence for small and large for gestational age babies was similar to that of the control group. Insulin m anagement Women with pre - gestational diabetes who are not using an insulin pump usually require multiple daily injections (MDI) with either short - acting human insulin or quick - acting analog insulin prior to each meal and an intermediate - acting human insulin or long - acting insulin analog before bedtime. In a rand- omized trial of women with pre - gestational diabetes which compared a MDI regimen with twice daily mixed insulin, the MDI regimen was associated with better glycemic control and less neonatal hypoglycemia [ ] . Both US and UK guidelines recom- mend the use of an MDI regimen for women with pre - gestational diabetes [ , ] . There are theoretical concerns around the use of insulin analogs in pregnancy and no study has yet been suf ciently powered to con rm their complete safety. Quick - acting insulin analogs have been widely used in diabetic pregnancy for over years without any evidence of any harm, and are recommended in both the US and UK guidelines. Outside pregnancy the use of rapid - acting insulin analogs (Lispro [Eli Lilly], Aspart [Novo Nordisk] and Glulisine Apidra [Sano - Aventis]) is beginning to replace short - acting human soluble insulin in MDI regimens, as they potentially offer better post - prandial glycemic control; however, their use puts greater The risk of severe hypoglycemia is less after the rst weeks of pregnancy when maternal insulin resistance rises and glucose variability and uctuations become less marked [ ] . Maternal mortality from hypoglycemia is extremely rare but well recognized [ ] and has been a recurrent cause of death in women with T1DM in the triennial Con dential Enquiry into Maternal Death in the UK over the last years. All women intensively managed with insulin need to be informed of the added risk of hypoglycemia in pregnancy and given advice to minimize the risk, including advice about whether it is safe to drive. For women with T1DM, their partner or a family member should be given instruction on when and how to give glucagon. Glucose m onitoring Accurate self - monitoring of blood glucose is required at least ve times a day throughout pregnancy. A study comparing blood glucose values obtained using self - monitoring of blood glucose and data from a continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) system found that unless the women were testing or more times a day many episodes where blood glucose values were outside the target range were missed [ ] . Monitoring should be increased for women on insulin pumps, those who are dose adjusting their insulin for each meal, experiencing hypoglycemia and when insulin doses are being changed. Glucose monitoring also needs to be carried out before driving and exercising. should be performed if nocturnal hypoglycemia is expected. For women already on insulin, their evening basal insulin is adjusted against their fasting glucose reading, and their bolus insulin against their hour post - prandial glucose value. A pre - bed reading may help to calculate the size of the snack required before bed. The use of a - hour post - prandial glucose value is more informative for adjusting insulin requirements than a pre - prandial measurement [ ] . In an randomized controlled trial of women with GDM, there were fewer large for gestational age babies, fewer cesarean sections for cephalo - pelvic disproportion and less neonatal hypoglycemia in women self - monitoring hour after a meal than immediately before [ ] . The use of CGM is currently too expensive for routine use in pregnancy; however, this is likely to change as CGM provides information on the extent of glucose uctuations and episodes of hyperglycemia and hypoglycemia undetected by self - monitoring of blood glucose [ , , ] . CGM performed at intervals of weeks during pregnancy was associated with a lower HbA 1c in the third trimester, lower birth weight and macrosomia compared with standard care in a rand- omized control trial of pre - gestational pregnant women [ ] . The use of CGM in clinical studies in pregnant women with T1DM has also highlighted the extent of glucose uctuations that are not detected by HbA 1c [ ] . Intensifying insulin regimens to strict and in exible glycemic targets based on HbA 1c may not be safe using the clinical tools and insulin currently available in routine care.

Diabetes in Pregnancy Chapter babies was collected over years in diabetic obstetric units in the UK. Insulin glargine was used prior to pregnancy in % of women, and stopped at booking in one patient. The bolus insulin used in this audit was insulin aspart ( %), lispro ( %) and human soluble in % of women. This audit identi ed no unex- pected or adverse maternal or fetal outcome [ ] , similar to earlier smaller clinical pregnancy studies [ , ] . The long - acting analog insulin detemir has no increased af n- ity binding to the IGF - I receptor [ ] and may be a more appro- priate basal insulin analog than glargine. The outcome of a large randomized control trial is awaited to show if it offers any clinical advantages in diabetic pregnancy. Continuous s ubcutaneous i nsulin p umps in p regnancy The continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion pumps (CSII) potentially offer the most physiologic form of subcutaneous insulin administration. The technology surrounding the CSII is progressing rapidly, with integrated insulin pumps and con- tinuous glucose monitor systems under development. There are no adequately powered multicentred studies evaluating the use of insulin pumps in pregnancy but clinical experience is increasing. Outside pregnancy the greatest bene t of insulin pumps over MDI on glycemic control is when control is poor beforehand; this bene t is less apparent when HbA 1c approaches the normal range [ ] . A systematic review of six randomized trials published between and of CSII involving pregnant women ( T1DM and T2DM) showed no signi cant differences in preg- nancy outcomes or glycemic control including hypoglycemic events between CSII and MDI [ ] . A Cochrane review that examined only two studies involving women with pregnan- cies also concluded that maternal glycemia and pregnancy out- comes were similar between women on CSII and MDI, and concluded more good quality studies were required [ ] . In one study that compared the use of CSII in insulin - treated pregnant women with T2DM and GDM with a matched control group on conventional insulin, pump use was associated with larger daily doses of insulin and greater weight gain, without any bene t on pregnancy outcomes. Large multicentered rand- omized controlled trials are required to identify which women would bene t from CSII in pregnancy. As HbA 1c values fall in pregnancy approaching the non - pregnancy range, it is likely that evaluating glycemic control in future trials using CSII will require continuous glucose monitoring systems as these will be more informative than HbA 1c values. CSII may improve overall control and glycemic uctuations in selected women with poorly controlled T1DM. In an Italian study that compared women with T1DM on CSII with women on MDI, there were no differences in glycemic control and preg- nancy outcomes between the groups; however, within the CSII group were women with more retinopathy and nephropathy in whom a poorer pregnancy outcome might have been predicted [ ] . reliance on the basal insulin for glycemic control between meals, for which the pharmacokinetic pro les of the longer - acting insulin analog (Glargine [Sano - Aventis] and Determir [Novo Nordisk]) are better suited than the intermediate human insulin (NPH [Neutral Protamine Hagedorn]). In some clinical studies, especially in T1DM, MDI regimens with insulin analogs are asso- ciated with less nocturnal hypoglycemia [ ] . Rapid - acting insulin analogs are being increasingly used by patients who carbohydrate count and adjust their insulin dose [ ] . For these reasons, MDI regimens with insulin analogs are attractive options for diabetic pregnancies. Increasingly, more women with T1DM are treated with insulin analogs prior to pregnancy and switching them to the older human soluble insulin in early pregnancy puts them at potential risk of worsening gly- cemic control at this critical time. Patient preference may also be important as the women receiving insulin aspart in a randomized controlled trial in pregnancy expressed greater patient satisfac- tion than women receiving human insulin [ ] . Recruiting women with T1DM to participate in future randomized studies will be increasingly dif cult as many will be reluctant to be ran- domized to the older human insulin. The clinical studies evaluating the ef cacy and safety of insulin lispro, the rst widely available rapid - acting insulin analog [ ] , all involve small numbers of women, are mostly ret- rospective and are inadequately designed or powered to evaluate serious adverse pregnancy outcomes. A multinational multicen- tered retrospective study of women with T1DM treated with insulin lispro for at least months before and months after conception and a control group of women treated with regular human insulin showed a trend towards fewer hypogly- cemic episodes and signi cantly greater reduction in HbA 1c during the rst trimester in the lispro arm [ ] . The early con- cerns that insulin lispro might be associated with an increased risk of retinopathy progression have not been substantiated by later studies. An open - labeled randomized control trial compared the safety and ef cacy of insulin aspart as part of a MDI regimen with NPH insulin with soluble human insulin in pregnant women with T1DM [ ] . Insulin aspart was associated with less nocturnal hypoglycemia and severe hypoglycemia and superior post - prandial glycemic control in the third trimester; there were no differences in other pregnancy outcomes between the insulins. Basal i nsulin a nalogs in p regnancy At present, neither basal analog insulin is recommended in the USA or UK national guidelines for use in pregnancy; instead human intermediate NPH insulin should be used times daily to provide adequate basal cover. Theoretical concerns persist around the long - acting analog insulin glargine because of its higher binding af nity for the IGF - I receptor and potential mitogenic potency compared with human insulin [ , ] . There have been no adequately controlled studies large enough to ascertain its safety in pregnancy. An audit of the use of insulin glargine in women with T1DM and

The mean blood glucose values were similar in both treat- ment arms and the incidence of maternal hypoglycemia was lower in the glibenclamide group ( % vs %). Perinatal outcome did not differ between the two groups [ ] . Oral hypoglycemic agents offer an attractive treatment option for diabetic obstetric clinics serving large numbers of women and their use will grow if future studies of the children of women with GDM treated with these oral agents show the children to be healthy and at no extra risk. Antenatal c are in a d iabetic p regnancy In addition to routine antenatal care, women with diabetes require extra maternal and fetal surveillance in pregnancy. Details in the obstetric and diabetic management of pregnant women with pre - gestational diabetes will vary according to country in which care is being given. The principles of management will be broadly similar and are outlined brie y below, according to trimester. Antenatal c are in the rst t rimester ( w eeks) All women should be seen at the earliest opportunity once preg- nancy is con rmed; this will be earlier than women seen in routine antenatal clinics. At the rst clinic visit it is important to review all prescribed and over - the - counter medications for safety and to ensure an appropriate dosage of folic acid has been prescribed. The importance of optimization of glycemic control during this time requires frequent contact and visits with the members of the multidisciplinary team. This is especially so for women with T2DM who may be starting insulin for the rst time. Dietary advice about the avoidance of hypoglycemia should be given, especially to women with T1DM [ ] . It is important to con rm viability of the pregnancy at weeks; this is before the routine screening for Down syndrome which occurs at weeks. The week ultrasound provides an accurate gestational age, which is important later in pregnancy when assessing fetal growth and the optimal timing of birth. The woman s current diet and levels of physical activity should be reviewed and advice given on appropriate food choices taken as snacks for the prevention and management of hypoglycemia. Advice on exercise should be individualized; however, as a general principle, women should be encouraged to take at least min/ day of moderate intensity physical activity [ ] . This may be modi- ed where there is a risk of exercise - induced hypoglycemia in women with T1DM. The general advice around diet remains the same as a non - diabetic pregnancy. The ADA guidelines on diet are more pre- scriptive than those of the UK NICE guidelines, with the former recommending g/day digestible carbohydrate and g/day ber [ ] . Women should be advised to spread their carbohydrate Oral h ypoglycemic a gents The use of oral agents in the management of diabetic pregnancies remains controversial: the ADA do not currently recommend their use, while the UK NICE guidelines endorse the use of met- formin in combination with insulin for the management of women with pre - existing T2DM, as well as metformin and glib- enclamide in the management of GDM [ , ] . The number of classes of oral hypoglycemic agents is increasing but the only oral hypoglycemic drugs that have been suf ciently evaluated in pregnancy are sulfonylureas and metformin. Metformin crosses the placenta, with fetal serum levels being comparable with maternal values [ ] ; however, there is no evidence of any increased risk for major malformations when metformin is taken during the rst trimester of pregnancy [ ] . The early introduction of metformin and glibenclamide in the 1970s in South Africa for women with T2DM and GDM was accompanied with a fall in neonatal mortality. Switching women from oral agents to insulin in early pregnancy was associated with better pregnancy outcomes than when women were treated with oral agents alone. In these early observational reports, poor gly- cemic control rather than oral hypoglycemic agents was thought to be the cause of the high malformation rates and perinatal mortality [ , ] . Many of the later studies in the literature are also observational and involve heterogeneous populations of pregnant women without adequate non - exposed controls, limit- ing the scope for meta - analysis to be undertaken [ ] . More robust clinical randomized trials of the use of metformin and glibenclamide in pregnancy have been conducted in women with GDM than in women with T2DM. A systematic review of four randomized controlled trials ( women) and ve obser- vational studies ( women) published prior to January of maternal and neonatal outcomes when GDM was treated with either glibenclamide or metformin found no differences in out- comes when compared with insulin [ ] . A randomized control trial of of women with GDM not controlled with diet alone by weeks gestation compared the ef cacy and safety of metformin with insulin [ ] . % were assigned to metformin and continued on metformin until the birth. Metformin was increased to a maximum of mg/day after which insulin was added in . % of women to the metformin if glycemic targets were not met. The mean fasting blood glucose was similar in the met- formin and insulin - treated groups but the - hour post - prandial blood glucose was lower in the metformin arm. The weight gain in late pregnancy was less in women treated with metformin and was associated with greater weight loss at weeks postpartum compared with women treated with insulin alone. Perinatal complications were broadly similar other than a lower incidence of severe hypoglycemia (blood glucose < . mmol/L) in the infants in the metformin group [ ] . % women in the metformin arm compared with . % of the insulin group stated that they would choose to receive their assigned treatment again in a future pregnancy.

Current UK obstetric guidelines are to offer induction of labor after completed weeks, or an elective cesarean section if indi- cated to women with both pre - gestational and GDM [ ] . The ADA consensus for the obstetric management of GDM puts greater emphasis on the estimated fetal weight when deciding the timing and mode of delivery [ , ] . The only reported randomized controlled trial of induction of labor versus expectant management involved women with insulin - treated diabetes and with GDM, who were all consid- ered to have uncomplicated pregnancies without macrosomia. Of the mothers in the expectant management group, % later required induction of labor for obstetric indications, with % having a cesarean delivery compared with % among the active induction group. More infants in the expectantly managed group were large for gestational age ( % vs %). Shoulder dystocia occurred in % of births to the women randomized to expectant management compared with none in the women randomized to induction of labor [ ] . A prospective case report study that compared an active policy of induction of labor at weeks gestation in women with insulin - treated diabetes with a previous cohort of women who were allowed to progress to spontaneous labor (n = ), reported an incidence of shoulder dystocia of . % among the women who were induced compared with . % in patients who gave birth beyond weeks gestation ( P < . In the CEMACH audit, the incidence of shoulder dystocia was two - to threefold higher and for Erb palsy more than - fold greater than that reported for the general UK antenatal popula- tion. Thirty - nine percent of women had induction of labor com- pared with % in the general maternity population and the cesarean section rate was also higher ( % vs %) [ ] . The preterm birth rate (birth before completed weeks gestation) is increased in diabetic pregnancies. In the CEMACH survey, . % of all births were preterm compared with . % in the general maternity population, with . % of all births being spontaneous preterm births [ ] . % of births in women with T1DM in the Netherlands between and were preterm [ ] . Recognized risk factors for spon- taneous preterm delivery in women with diabetes are pre - eclampsia [ ] , nephropathy, poor glycemic control preterm delivery [ ] and obesity [ ] . It is routine clinical obstetric practice to give steroids (betamethasone or dexamethasone) as two intramuscular injections hours apart to mothers at risk of a preterm birth to accelerate fetal lung maturity. Although not contraindicated in women with diabetes, their use is associated with a worsening of glycemic control, and it is therefore impor- tant to increase insulin doses proactively or treat the woman with an insulin infusion to supplement her basal bolus insulin regimen [ , ] . Glycemic m anagement d uring l abor There is an association between maternal hyperglycemia during labor and neonatal hypoglycemia [ , ] , and so it is at around weeks for GDM, with the WHO criteria for IGT being used to establish the diagnosis [ ] . The ADA guidelines advocate an initial - g glucose challenge test at weeks with a glucose measurement hour later. Women with a positive challenge test, de ned as an - hour plasma glucose > . mmol/L then proceed to a diagnostic OGTT [ ] . The ADA guidelines were based on a consensus document last modi ed in and are likely to change following the pub- lication of the HAPO trial [ ] . Management of g estational d iabetes The glycemic management for women with GDM was discussed previously. All women diagnosed with GDM need to be taught to self - monitor their blood glucose concentrations, and perform daily tests fasting and - hour after meals. The glycemic targets for GDM are identical to those for pre - gestational diabetes. Initial management is with diet and exercise, to which many women will respond. If glycemic targets are not met within weeks, or glyc- emic levels are considered too high to be controlled adequately with diet and exercise alone, hypoglycemic therapy is required. Increased physical activity combined with dietary modi cation and intensive glycemic management reduces perinatal complica- tions in women with GDM [ ] . Small clinical studies have reported that exercise programs using upper arm exercises and resistance training reduce fasting and post - prandial blood glucose values in obese women with GDM, as well as reducing insulin requirements when compared with women treated with diet alone [ , ] . The use of metformin and glibenclaimide is not recommended by the ADA [ ] , but has been endorsed in the UK NICE guide- lines [ ] following the publications of two randomized control trials of their use in the management of GDM [ , ] . Recent case controlled studies of metformin [ ] and glibenclaimide [ ] in specialized antenatal clinics where insulin therapy is available if glycemic targets are not met suggest these agents have a role in the pharmacologic management of GDM. Both drugs are well tolerated, and in the case of metformin is associated with less maternal weight gain than insulin [ , ] . Patient prefer- ence for oral therapies rather than insulin is likely to increase their future use as rst - line pharmacologic therapy after diet and exer- cise for the management of GDM. The observation that the rate of stillbirth rose from weeks gestation to term prompted Peel and Oakley [ ] to advocate delivery at weeks in . By , some obstetric units were allowing selected women with T1DM and uncompli- cated diabetic pregnancies to go into spontaneous labor irrespec- tive of the gestational age. Diabetes f ollowing g estational d iabetes; s creening and p revention Women with GDM are at increased risk of developing diabetes in the future. Indeed, the original O Sullivan criteria for diagnos- ing GDM were based on the risk of future diabetes in the mother [ , ] . While the majority of women who go on to develop diabetes have T2DM, a smaller minority will develop T1DM [ , ] . Progression from IGT to diabetes is more common in women with a history of GDM [ ] . In the control arm of the Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP), of the parous women had a prior history of GDM while had no history. Both groups had equiv- alent degrees of IGT at baseline but within years of randomiza- tion, despite the women with a history of GDM being younger, % had developed diabetes compared with . % among the women without a history of GDM. The rate of progression to diabetes following a GDM preg- nancy depends on the original criteria for its diagnosis and the length of follow - up [ ] . The risk factors for the progression to diabetes are as follow [ , ] : Family origin with high prevalence of diabetes (e.g. South Asian, Afro - Caribbean, Middle Eastern); Treatment with insulin in pregnancy; Maternal obesity; Weight gain postpartum; and Family history of diabetes. In Latin American women, parity has also been identi ed as a risk factor for T2DM [ ] . The rate of progression is quicker in obese women with high pregnancy fasting blood glucose values and who have an insulinopenic response to oral glucose postpartum [ , , ] . The time interval between a GDM pregnancy and future T2DM has decreased in recent years because of increasing obesity. The prevalence of diabetes at years among two Danish cohorts diagnosed with GDM between and rose from . Lifestyle intevention, metformin and thiazolidinediones have all been shown in women with a history of GDM to slow the progression to T2DM [ , , ] . A subanalysis of the DPP found that the women with prior GDM who received inten- sive lifestyle ( ) or metformin treatment ( ) had a % and % less risk of progression to diabetes within years than the women assigned to the placebo arm. The corresponding risk reductions in the parous women without a history of GDM were % and % for intensive lifestyle and metformin, respec- tively. This suggests that a history of GDM identi es a group of women particularly susceptible to the protective effects of met- formin. The DPP investigators estimated that only ve to six women with a history of GDM and IGT would need to be treated for years with metformin or lifestyle intervention to prevent one case of diabetes [ ] . important to maintain normoglycemia ( mmol/L) during labor [ ] . To maintain this, most women with T1DM will require treatment with an insulin infusion with variable dosing from the onset of established labor. The use of insulin pumps in labor has not yet been subjected to critical evaluation, but early observational studies would suggest they may have a place [ ] . The need for intravenous insulin for all women with GDM during labor and birth is less certain. A retrospective study of singleton pregnancies of women with GDM ( insulin - treated) in Australia reported that ( %) of the women who were diet - controlled and ( %) of the women treated with insulin were able to maintain their blood glucose < mmol/L throughout the peripartum period without insulin [ ] . Immediately following birth, insulin requirement falls precipi- tously to below the pre - pregnancy levels. Clear instructions need to be given in the clinical notes to reduce the insulin at this time and to monitor blood glucose hourly. Postnatal m anagement Breastfeeding Breastfeeding provides health advantages for both mother and child. In the offspring of mothers without diabetes, breastfeeding protects against early childhood obesity [ , ] . In the Nurses Health Study, breastfeeding during the rst months was inversely associated with overweight in adolescence. This associa- tion was also seen among mothers with diabetes during preg- nancy ( with GDM and with pre - gestational diabetes) as well as offspring of normal - weight women (n = ) and over- weight women (n = ) without diabetes during pregnancy [ ] . In a case control study of young people with T2DM and matched control subjects aged years, fewer people with T2DM had been breastfed for any duration than control subjects ( . % for African - Americans, . % for Latino and . % for whites of Northern European ancestry). The overall crude odds ratio for the association of breastfeeding (ever compared with never) and T2DM was . Earlier concerns that the advantages of breastfeeding on child- hood weight might be lost if the mother has diabetes because her milk is more calorie dense have not been substantiated by later studies [ ] . All women with diabetes should be encouraged and supported to breastfeed if this is their wish. For women with T1DM, there is an increased risk of hypoglycemia in the postnatal period espe- cially when breastfeeding; they should be advised to have a meal or snack available before or during feeds. For women with GDM there is no risk of hypoglycemia during breastfeeding, not least because all hypoglycemic treatment should be stopped immediately after birth. If maternal blood glucose remains high and oral agents are needed, metformin and glibenclamide are the only ones currently recommended when breastfeeding [ ] .
